coredata.dc:title: Investigation of the adsorption of a DNA based purine derivative on N/B-doped coronene and coronene by means of DFT and NCI interaction analysis
coredata.dc:creator: S.Al-Otaibi, Jamelah
Mary, Y.Sheena
Mary, Y.Shyma
Mondal, Asmita
Acharjee, Nivedita
Balachandar, S.
coredata.dc:description: With the aid of density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT simulations, the adsorption of 2-amino-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one (ADO) on coronene and coronene that has been doped with boron and nitrogen has been investigated. While conducting this study, the binding energy, charge analysis, orbital analysis, quantum theory of atom in molecules (QTAIM) and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) were taken into account. The coronene sheet serves as an electron acceptor while the nucleophilic portion of ADO acts as an electron-donor, causing intermolecular interaction with a focus on the reactive region. With the exception of coronene, all the complexes have negative Gibbs free energy changes due to ADO adsorption. Furthermore, enthalpy changes have low values for coronene complex (−5.18 kcal/mol) and large values for doped complexes (−255.56 to −284. 61 kcal/mol). Our calculations demonstrate that the most stable complex among the other systems under study. Is the ADO with nitrogen parallel doped complex, with a binding energy of −284.13 kcal/mol. Other doped complexes have lower energies with the pristine coronene complex having the lowest value. The NH, NH2, CO and ring modes of ADO possess more Raman intensity in the after adsorption and it is due to the enhancement mechanism due to SERS effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DFT
SERS
Doped coronene
Reactivity analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their gratitude to Princess Nourah Bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2022R13), Princess Nourah Bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized geometry and electrostatic potential maps of coronene: (a) GG (b) GBB (c) GNN (d), GBNP (e) GBNO.
Optimized geometry of (a) ADO (b) HOMO (c) LUMO (d) MEP plot.
Optimized geometry of ADO with (a) GG (b) GBB (c) GNN (d) GBNP (e) GBNO complexes.
MEP plots of ADO with (a) GG (b) GBB (c) GNN (d) GBNP (e) GBNO complexes.
HOMO-LUMO plots of (a) GG (b) GBB (c) GNN (d), GBNP (e) GBNO.
HOMO-LUMO plots of ADO with (a) GG (b) GBB (c) GNN (d) GBNP (e) GBNO complexes.
AIM-RDG isosurfaces (isovalue = 0.75), in the range −0.04 < sign(λ2)ρ < 0.04 a.u. BCPs are shown in magenta colour and paths in orange colour.
Energies, Dipole moments, polarizability and binding energy values of ADO, coronene and ADO-coronene complexes.
Chemical descriptors of ADO and ADO-coronene complexes (eV).

head.ce:title: Investigation of the adsorption of a DNA based purine derivative on N/B-doped coronene and coronene by means of DFT and NCI interaction analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Princess Nourah Bint Abdulrahman University, P.O. Box 84428, Riyadh 11671, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: PG and Research Department of Chemistry
Vivekanandha College of Arts and Sciences for Women (Autonomous)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: PG and Research Department of Chemistry, Vivekanandha College of Arts and Sciences for Women (Autonomous), Elayampalayam, Namakkal, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the aid of density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT simulations, the adsorption of 2-amino-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one (ADO) on coronene and coronene that has been doped with boron and nitrogen has been investigated. While conducting this study, the binding energy, charge analysis, orbital analysis, quantum theory of atom in molecules (QTAIM) and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) were taken into account. The coronene sheet serves as an electron acceptor while the nucleophilic portion of ADO acts as an electron-donor, causing intermolecular interaction with a focus on the reactive region. With the exception of coronene, all the complexes have negative Gibbs free energy changes due to ADO adsorption. Furthermore, enthalpy changes have low values for coronene complex (−5.18 kcal/mol) and large values for doped complexes (−255.56 to −284. 61 kcal/mol). Our calculations demonstrate that the most stable complex among the other systems under study. Is the ADO with nitrogen parallel doped complex, with a binding energy of −284.13 kcal/mol. Other doped complexes have lower energies with the pristine coronene complex having the lowest value. The NH, NH2, CO and ring modes of ADO possess more Raman intensity in the after adsorption and it is due to the enhancement mechanism due to SERS effect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The most extensively studied metal free electro-catalytics during the past years are graphene and its derivatives [1]. Numerous derivatives have been referred to as electro-catalytics for degrading pollutants since metal catalyst’s extensive commercialization has been hindered by their pricey construction and corrosive instability [2]. The majority of classic chemotherapeutic drugs have significant side effects during patient treatment even though chemotherapy is widely utilized cancer treatments [3]. In order to deliver chemotherapy medications specifically into tumor tissues, many investigations have been carried out to look into efficient drug delivery systems [4,5]. A fascinating area of this study, nanotechnology has many potential uses. Recently, many nanoscale drug carriers have been utilized for the targeted delivery of medications to treat cancers [6]. Nano-sized carbon allotropes have shown promise in nano-medicine, notably as drug carriers [7]. Recently, fascinating fluorescent probes with novel properties, including broad band absorption, strong chemical and high photo bleaching stability, good biocompatibility, and low cyto-toxicity, have been developed using graphene quantum dots, including doped ones [8–11].

Pharmaceutical and personal care products have caused considerable concern as aquatic contaminants due to their influence on the environmental and poor degrading performance in sewage treatment plants [12]. Effective treatment methods are therefore essential in lowering the presence of contaminants in aquatic habitats. One of the most cutting-edge oxidation techniques currently in use, electro catalytic oxidation of metal-free materials, may effectively avoid secondary contamination, highlighting the importance of electrode material section. The most researched of these materials during the past years has been graphene and its variants [13]. According to theoretical and experimental investigations, doping of nitrogen, which may substitute for random carbon atoms, interact with vacancies in the lattice can be used to influence the electronic and chemical properties of carbon materials [14–17]. Due to its identical atomic size and five valence electrons for interacting with carbon, nitrogen has frequently been used for doping of nano material [18]. It is possible to change graphene’s band gap and give it fresh properties for device applications by doping it with substituted N atoms [19]. Much emphasis has been paid to the heteroatom doping of boron, fluorine, phosphorus, nitrogen and sulfur in graphene to enhance its electro catalytic stability and dispersion [20–24]. With a large number of holes in B-doped graphene, carrier concentration and hence graphene conductivity are improved [25]. Because it has lower electro negativity than nitrogen and produces p-type conductivity in graphene, boron is a suitable dopant material [26]. There have been a number of published earlier works on the creation of B-doped graphene nano particles [27–29]. The longevity of Pd clusters supported by pure, B-doped, and defective graphene quantum dots, as well as their reactivity toward oxygen adsorption, were theoretically examined by Montejo-Alvaro et al. [30]. Li et al. claim that graphene doped with nitrogen exhibits a blue luminescence as opposed to their N-free counterpart’s green luminescence [31]. Under blue light illumination, Juang et al. found that boron doped graphene quantum dots emit extremely fluorescent green and red emissions [32]. Several investigations on the adsorption of drugs with doped and undoped graphene have been reported [33–44].

The genetic materials known as deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) have a complicated biomolecular structure that includes four separate nitrogenous bases [45]. The DNA based applications include, diagnosis, characterization and medicine [46,47]. The significant DNA purine bases, guanine and adenine, are essential for the biological synthesis of proteins, cerebral blood flow, cell signaling and even serving as a repository for biological knowledge about heredity [48]. The immune system becomes weakened and mutated due to aberrant guanine and adenine modifications in nucleic acids, which can result in a variety of diseases such as cancer, epilepsy, Parkinson’s, AIDS and liver ailments [49]. Therefore, it has become crucial for clinical diagnostics to simultaneously determine these purine bases. One of the most useful techniques for examining changes in properties of materials for biosensor applications is the interaction of DNA nucleotides at various levels in research [50,51]. The interaction of guanine (2-amino-1,7-dihydropurin-6-one: ADO) molecule with graphene and N/B-doped graphene is examined in the current study using DFT analysis.

N/B-doped graphene can display a variety of behaviors, as evidences by experiments, with different doping types and positions. To analyze the role of doping in drug delivery systems, four distinct graphene structures with N/B doping at center are introduced. In pristine nanosurfaces, the bond lengths are: CC = 1.43 Å (GG), 1.42 to 1.47 Å (GBB), 1.36 to 1.46 Å (GNN), 1.43 Å (GBNO), 1.37 to 1.42 Å (GBNP); C-B = 1.53 Å (GBB), 1.49 Å (GBNO and GBNP); CN = 1.41 Å (GNN), 1.41 to 1.43 Å (GBNO and GBNP).

The ADO molecule is initially aligned parallel to the nano surfaces at a distance of less than 3 Å for getting maximum interaction, and then extensive geometric optimization is carried out. Fig. 3 displays the GG-ADO complexes’s most stable geometries. The CO and N2H12 groups of ADO molecule are near to surface of GBB and GBNO with a separation of 2.52, 2.40 Å and 2.43, 2.31 Å from the surface with an edge on adsorption, respectively (Fig. 3). For the GBNP and GNN systems, the interaction is carried out by CO, N3H13 and NH2 groups, and the ADO molecule is oriented nearly perpendicularly to the nanosurface. The separations between ADO molecule and the nanosurfaces are: 3.04 Å (CO), 2.95 Å (NH2) and 2.38 Å (NH) for GBNP and 2.76 Å (CO), 2.27 Å (NH) and 2.94 Å (NH2) for GNN systems. In GG system, CO and N3H13 are near to the grapheme with separation of 2.65 Å (CO) and 2.68 Å (NH). After ADO adsorption on nanosurfaces there is a no change for CC length in GG nanosurface while the CC length becomes 1.41 to 1.43 Å in GBB, 1.38 to 1.43 in GNN, 1.42 to 1.44 Å in GBNO and 1.38 to 1.43 Å in GBNP systems. The CN bond lengths becomes 1.40 to 1.44 Å in GNN, 1.39 to 1.42 Å in GBNO and 1.40 to 1.42 Å in GBNP and the B-C bond lengths becomes 1.53 to 1.55 Å in GBB, 1.49 to 1.50 Å in GBNO and 1.49 to 1.51 Å in GBNP systems.

Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps have been used to analyze the binding sites in order to better comprehend them. For this, Figs. 1 and 4 depict the MEPs of pristine and N/B-doped graphene, respectively. While the red portions have a high concentration of electrons, the blue regions have a low concentration of them. The most reactive spots in ADO molecule are hydrogen (blue) and O1, N4, N5 atoms (red and yellow) (Fig. 2). All the ring carbon atoms of GG, center ring containing C and B atoms of GBB, outer ring containing B and C atoms GBN rings have an abundance of electrons where as for GNN outer carbon atoms are yellow and red and are electron rich sites (Fig. 1). For GNN the centre part is blue having a low concentration of electrons and for other coronene doped systems the hydrogen atoms are blue with low concentration of electrons [57,58]. As can be observed from Fig. 3 the adsorption distances of ADO from the nanocarriers shows that the interactions are non-covalent [59]. After ADO adsorption on nanosurfaces, as shown in MEP plots in Fig. 4, the distribution of electron rich and deficient regions is redistributed, demonstrating charge transfer in the system. In ADO-adsorbed complexes, reactive sites are modified as: In GG, all C atoms become yellow while O1, N4 and N5 of ADO are still red and H atoms blue. Modification is observed for the distribution of potential in ADO adsorbed GBB with more red and yellow in GG and bluish on ADO; In ADO adsorbed GNN, centre of N containing graphene’s potential diminishes with slight yellow while O1 and N5 of ADO become more red, and similar nature is observed in ADO-GBNP. For ADO-GBNO, ring with B becomes more red and yellow and O1, N4 and N5 also become more red and yellow. This modification of reactive sites is due to chemisorptions and non-uniform distribution of charges according to Mulliken’s charge analysis [60].

The dipole moment (DM) estimates prior to and following drug adsorption on the nanocarrier surfaces (Table 1). The GBNO and GBNP are polar, with DM of 4.5607 and 4.6482 Debye respectively, while the GG, GBB and GNN structures are non-polar (zero dipole moments). The dipole moment values rise after the ADO binds to the nanocarrier surfaces, suggesting that the graphene-ADO complexes are soluble in an aqueous media. The polarizability of all complexes is higher than that of pristine nanosurfaces and significantly higher that of ADO molecule, demonstrating charge transfer in the complexes with enhanced nonlinear optical activity. Here also maximum polarizability is found for identical para substitution systems [64,65].

To better understand the impact of the nanocarriers and the ADO adsorption on GG, the energies of FMOs and band gaps are computed (Table 3 ). The energy gap of the GG was measured to be 4.0833 eV which is consistent with earlier results (4.32 eV) [66]. Table 3 displays that all N/B doped GG have small gaps in comparison with to that of coronene so that GG (4.0833 eV) > GBNO (2.8155 eV) > GBNP (2.7826 eV) > GNN(0.3442 eV) > GBB (0.3064 eV) and similar B or N atoms doping in the para position of the coronene ring show minimum energy gap. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap values alter as a result of the ADO adsorption on the nanocarrier surfaces: while adsorption of ADO on dissimilar doping only causes a minor change in the energy gap, GBB/GNN exhibits a significant shift (increase). The FMOs energy levels are also changed as a result of the adsorption of ADO. After adsorption on the GG, the ADO’s DOS spectra (Fig. S1) changes significantly, whereas the nanocarriers’ DOS curve very little [67–69].

Chemical descriptors of ADO and their complexes are estimated to account for the reactivity of the nanocarriers (Table 3). When ADO adsorbs on nanocarrier surfaces, the chemical potential of the nanocarriers is raised. While adsorption on the surfaces of doped nanosurfaces, there is an increase in hardness value for all except for GG adsorption. The ADO is stable, as seen by the electrophilicity index low value (2.2491 eV). All electrophilicity index values increases with ADO adsorption. The simulations demonstrate that ADO adsorption on nanocarriers enhances the chemical stability overall [70]. Figs. 5 and 6
display the FMOs plot of pristine nanosurfaces and nanosurface that have been adsorbed with ADO molecule. For GG, electron density are not seen along the axial C atoms (HOMO and in LUMO); For GBB, HOMO is mainly on the B atom containing centre ring while LUMO is absent in carbon atoms at center ring; For GNN, HOMO density is throughout the molecule and no electron density is seen along the N, N containing axis for LUMO; Electron density of LUMO is absent in B atom in GBNO and HOMO is absent in N atom of GBNP (Fig. 6). HOMO is over the ADO in GG and dissimilar substituted systems while it is over the coroenene for identical B/N substitution rings. LUMO is over the coroenene for all systems except for GBB system where it is over the ADO (Fig. 6). These electron densities of FMOs demonstrate maximum charge transfer and adsorption process.

Table S1 shows the Mulliken charges of the molecule’s atoms before and after adsorption, showing variations for molecule’s charges that are close to nanosurfaces: O1, N2 for GBB and GBNO, O1, N3, N6 for GBNP and GNN systems and O1, N3 for the GG system show changes for the charge values. The charge of O1 in ADO is −0.4567e which becomes more negative in the doped systems with maximum value of −0.5578e in GBNP system. The charge of N2 becomes less negative −0.6636 for GBB and −0.6648 for GBNO after adsorption while the corresponding value of ADO is −0.7265e. The charge of N3 in GBNP and GNN shows a same trend while that N6 becomes more negative due to adsorption process. Adsorption in GG system produces a small change for N3 charge from −0.7326 (ADO) to −0.7456 in complex. The hydrogen atoms, H12, H13, H15 and H16 near to the nanosurfaces also show a variation in value due to chemisorptions.

For BN doped systems, the TD-DFT yield two adsorptions at 433.33, 356.18 nm (GBNP) and 481.12, 389.29 nm (GBNO) while for the other two doped systems the adsorptions are single valued and at higher values: 567.43 nm for GBB and 631.16 nm for GNN. For GG with ADO, there is a slight increase for the adsorption from 253.87 nm (ADO) to 264.36 nm. Following adsorption, the wavelength shifts to the middle of the visible range for the B/N doped systems in comparison with that of GG system (table S2, Fig.S2).

Fig.S3 shows the ADO’s DFT predicted Raman spectra both before and after adsorption. Most of the ADO molecule’s vibrational modes are amplified following the adsorption procedure due to SERS, according to a comparison of intensities [71]. Additionally, the ADO’s inactive Raman modes are active and have increased intensity in the ADO-nanocarrier complexes’ spectrum. Especially NH, NH2, CO and ring modes possess more Raman intensity in the after adsorption. Maximum enhancement is observed for similar doped atoms of B/N which support the values of adsorption energy and the chemisorptions of the complexes.

Interactions present in the studied complexes were analyzed by calculation of the QTAIM (Quantum Theory of atoms-in-molecules) by Bader et al. [72,73] which revealed non-covalent interactions [74] (NCIs) between GG and ADO, and were accordingly characterized by visualization of the reduced density gradient (RDG) isosurfaces. The bond critical points (BCPs), ring critical points (RCPs) and paths in the AIM-RDG isosurfaces of GBB, GBNO, GBNP, GG and GNN structures are represented in Fig. 7 .

GBB, GBNO, GBNP, GG and GNN show the presence of three BCPs between the cage and the drug molecule associated with the NCIs between oxygen atom of the drug and carbon atom of the cage and two N-hydrogen atoms of the drug with the carbon atom of the cage. These three BCPs show total electron density (ED) between 0.005 and 0.013 au, positive Laplacian of electron density between 0.015 and 0.052 au and LOL between 0.054 and 0.184 au. Two RCPs are also observed in each case between the cage and the drug molecule showing total electron density of 0.002–0.003 au, positive Laplacian of ED between 0.012 and 0.018 au and LOL between 0.039 and 0.068 au. Note that red and blue colored regions associated with the strongly attractive and strongly repulsive NCIs are observed around boron atoms in the GBB, GBNO and GBNP structures. The cage structure shows seven RCPs, while the drug molecule shows two RCPs associated the aryl rings in each case, identified as the repulsive NCIs in the respective isosurfaces [75].

Analysis of the electron localization function [76,77] (ELF) allows obtaining a representation of structure in chemical systems. The ELF of each structure (Fig. S4) shows two V(O) and V'(O) monosynaptic basin with 5.71 e, 5.95 e, 5.95 e, 6.03 e and 5.95 e for GBB, GBNO, GBNP, GG and GNN respectively with the non-bonding electron density (NBED) at the oxygen atom, while the minimal difference in GBB, GBNO and GG suggest the influence of complexation with the cage comprising of boron, nitrogen and carbon atoms. The B-C bonding regions present in GBB, GBNO and GBNP integrate the total population between 2.82 e and 3.00 e indicative of the overpopulated single bonds in each case. The NC bonding regions integrate the total population of 2.00–2.22 e in GBNO, 2.04–2.10 e in GBNP and 1.89–2.22 e in GNN, while the nitrogen atom present inside the cage shows V(N) and V'(N) basins with 0.785, 1.079 and 1.457 e in GBNO, GBNP and GNN respectively associated with NBED [78,79].

The molecular electrostatic potential [80] (MEP) maps were analyzed to characterize the reactive zones in the studied complexes (Fig. S5). The MEP isosurface of GBB shows the presence of 9 minima and 12 maxima with the electrostatic potential (ESP) between −206.32 and 217.02 kcal mol−1. The blue regions associated with highly negative ESP between −206.32 and 100.48 kcal mol−1 are located around the cage structure, while the red regions associated with highly positive ESP between 164.10 and 217.02 kcal mol−1 are located near the drug molecule. The green regions located around the cage and the drug molecule are associated with ESP between 100.48 and 115 kcal mol−1. The MEP isosurface of GBNO shows the presence of 8 minima and 18 maxima with the electrostatic potential (ESP) between −87.17 and 88.15 kcal mol−1. Traces of blue regions associated with negative ESP between −87.17 and −43.34 kcal mol−1 and red regions associated with positive ESP between 44.32 and 88.15 kcal mol−1 are located near the cage as well as the ADO molecule, while maximum area of the isosurface is covered by the green region associated with the ESP value between −21.42 and 40.00 kcal mol−1. The MEP isosurface of GBNP shows similitude with GBNO, with the blue regions associated with negative ESP values between −91.70 and −50.30 kcal mol−1, red regions associated with highly positive ESP values between 32.51 and 73.91 kcal mol−1 and the green regions with ESP values between −30.00 and 11.81 kcal mol−1. The MEP isosurface of GG shows green coloured region spread over almost the entire isosurface with ESP values between −44.00 and 36.18 kcal mol−1. The isosurface of GNN shows blue regions near the drug molecule associated with the negative ESP between −86.52 and −42.84 kcal mol−1, while a smal region covering two maxima with ESPs at + 85.97 and + 88.19 kcal mol−1 located at the middle of the cage. The green region spread over the isosurface is associated with the ESP between −25.00 and 44.00 kcal mol−1. Note that the presence of two boron atoms in GBB remarkably changes the range of ESP relative to the other structures. The ESP range of GBNO differs minimally relative to GBNP, owing to the varied disposition of boron and nitrogen atoms [81].

A theoretical investigation of the adsorption of ADO on coronene surface applying DFT simulations was performed. Different properties were utilized to estimate the phenomenon of ADO adsorption. ADO-GNN complex was found to be the most stable one with binding energy of −284.13 kcal/mol. The polarizability of all complexes is greater than that of pristine nanosurfaces and significantly higher that of ADO molecule, showing charge transfer in the complexes with enhanced non-linear optical (NLO) activity. Intensity enhancement is observed for similar doped atoms of B/N which support the values of adsorption energy and the chemisorptions of the complexes. The presence of two boron atoms in GBB remarkably changes the range of potential values with respect to the other structures. The ESP range of GBNO differs slightly relative to GBNP, owing to the varied disposition of boron and nitrogen atoms.

Jamelah S.Al-Otaibi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Validation. Y.Sheena Mary: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Validation. Y.Shyma Mary: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Validation. Asmita Mondal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Validation. Nivedita Acharjee: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Validation. S. Balachandar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their gratitude to Princess Nourah Bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number(PNURSP2022R13), Princess Nourah Bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2022.120373.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrapure graphene is a poor electrocatalytic: definitive proof of the key role of metallic impurities in graphene-based electrocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.Mazanek, J.Luxa, S.Matejkova, J.Kucera, D.Sedmidubsky, M.Pumera, Z.Sofer, Ultrapure graphene is a poor electrocatalytic: definitive proof of the key role of metallic impurities in graphene-based electrocatalysis, ACS Nano 13 (2019) 1374-1582, https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.8b07534

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.Bi, J.Lin, Y.Liu, H.He, F.Huang, Y.Cao, Dehydrogenation of formic acid at room temperature: boosting palladium nanoparticle efficiency by coupling with pyridinic-nitrogen-doped carbon, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 55 (2016) 11849-11853, https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201605961

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomedicine: An effective tool in cancer therapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective fluorescence determination of the hematin level in human erythrocytes with no need for separation from bulk hemoglobin
